tldr: Headlines across 14 buckets split into two dominant frames: UK and anglophone wire outlets headline a TikTok investigation or compliance probe, while broader international outlets foreground the UK overnight curfew proposal and EU age-restriction deliberations as new policy actions. The sole outlier — Sixth Tone — is the only headline to challenge whether bans actually protect children.

evidence.quote: UK proposes voluntary overnight social media curfew
EU experts urge social media restrictions for kids under 13
Gov't Looking to Restrict Social Media Subscription

frames.sub_frame: TikTok probe for child safety compliance

evidence.quote: UK investigates TikTok over child safety measures
TikTok under investigation in UK over child safety measures
UK probes TikTok's child safety measures
EU lawmakers want to turn off addictive social media features
'Childhood Won't Wait…': EU Chief Weighs Age Restrictions
TikTok fails to protect children from harmful content

frames.sub_frame: bans alone insufficient for child safety

evidence.quote: Social Media Bans Alone Won't Protect Kids

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only headline to surface the curfew's opt-out clause ('but users can opt out'), flagging a voluntary/non-binding nature that every other curfew headline omits — most of which use 'propose' or 'impose' without qualification.
Uses 'impose' and 'latest online crackdown' rather than 'propose', rendering the UK curfew as compulsory enforcement — the most coercive headline framing in the corpus.
Only headline to deploy urgent child-welfare language ('Childhood Won't Wait…'), foregrounding emotional stakes over regulatory process.
Sole headline to challenge the regulatory consensus: 'Bans Alone Won't Protect Kids' positions a Chinese academic counter-finding against the global crackdown wave visible in all other titles.

bottom_line: The headline layer frames child social media regulation as settled enforcement — TikTok under probe, curfews being imposed — with only one headline globally (Sixth Tone) questioning whether bans are the right instrument.
